scenes.title: The Circle and Helix
scenes.description: A circle in the xy-plane is defined by $x = \cos t$, $y = \sin t$. Adding a $z$-component that grows with $t$ unwinds the circle into a helix ascending through space.
scenes.markdown:
# The Circle and Helix

A **parametric curve** in 3D assigns a point $(x, y, z)$ to each value of a parameter $t$:

$$\mathbf{r}(t) = \bigl(x(t),\; y(t),\; z(t)\bigr)$$

## The Circle

The unit circle in the $xy$-plane is:

$$\mathbf{r}(t) = (\cos t,\; \sin t,\; 0), \quad t \in [0, 2\pi]$$

Every point stays at height $z = 0$.

## The Helix

Lift the circle by letting $z$ grow proportionally to $t$:

$$\mathbf{r}(t) = \left(\cos t,\; \sin t,\; \frac{t}{2\pi}\right), \quad t \in [0, 4\pi]$$

After one full revolution ($t = 2\pi$) the curve has risen by 1 unit. This is a **right circular helix**.

## Pitch

The **pitch** $h$ controls how fast the helix rises per revolution:

$$\mathbf{r}(t) = \left(\cos t,\; \sin t,\; \frac{h\,t}{2\pi}\right)$$

- $h = 0$: flat circle
- $h = 1$: rises 1 unit per turn
- $h = 3$: steeply rising helix

The arc length of one turn is $\sqrt{(2\pi)^2 + h^2}$.

> **Tip**: Use the pitch slider to watch the circle smoothly stretch into a helix.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide isometric view — see the circle and helix together in 3D

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view — see the helix projected as concentric rings from above

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view — see the helix height along Z

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view of the helix

steps.title: Draw the unit circle
steps.description: The unit circle $\mathbf{r}(t) = (\cos t, \sin t, 0)$ traces a flat circle in the $xy$-plane.

steps.title: Rise into a helix
steps.description: Add $z = t/(2\pi)$. The curve spirals upward through two full turns, rising 2 units total.

steps.title: Compare circle and helix
steps.description: Both curves share the same $xy$ projection but the helix climbs upward. Rotate to see the difference from the top vs. side view.

steps.title: Interactive: adjust the pitch
steps.description: Drag the pitch slider $h$. At $h=0$ you get a flat circle; larger values produce a steeper helix.

scenes.prompt: Teach parametric curves as motion over t, emphasizing projection vs height evolution. Use the pitch slider to connect algebraic z(t) to geometric rise per turn.

scenes.title: Lissajous Figures
scenes.description: Lissajous curves $x = \sin(at + \varphi)$, $y = \sin(bt)$ arise when two perpendicular oscillations have different frequencies $a$ and $b$.
scenes.markdown:
# Lissajous Figures

A **Lissajous figure** is the path traced by a point undergoing two independent sinusoidal oscillations at right angles:

$$x(t) = \sin(a\,t + \varphi), \quad y(t) = \sin(b\,t), \quad z(t) = 0$$

## Frequency Ratio

The shape depends on the ratio $a : b$:

| $a$ | $b$ | Shape |
|-----|-----|-------|
| 1 | 1 | Circle / ellipse |
| 2 | 1 | Figure-eight |
| 3 | 2 | Pretzel / bow-tie |
| 3 | 1 | Triple loop |

When $a/b$ is rational the curve closes after finite time. When irrational it fills a rectangle densely.

## Phase Shift $\varphi$

The phase $\varphi$ rotates and deforms the figure:

- $\varphi = 0$: straight line (when $a = b$)
- $\varphi = \pi/2$: circle (when $a = b = 1$)

## Applications

Lissajous figures appear in:
- **Electronics**: visualizing phase and frequency on oscilloscopes
- **Mechanics**: coupled pendula
- **Acoustics**: visualizing harmonic ratios

> **Tip**: Try $a=3, b=2, \varphi=\pi/4$ for a beautiful pretzel shape.

views.name: Face On
views.description: Wider face-on view — see the full Lissajous figure in the XY plane

views.name: Iso
views.description: 3D perspective — notice the Lissajous figure has no Z extent

views.name: Top Down
views.description: Looking straight down from above

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view along X — see the figure edge-on

steps.title: $a=1, b=1$: Circle
steps.description: When $a = b = 1$ and $\varphi = \pi/2$, the Lissajous curve is a perfect circle.

steps.title: $a=2, b=1$: Figure-eight
steps.description: Doubling the $x$-frequency creates the classic figure-eight. The curve has 2 horizontal lobes.

steps.title: $a=3, b=2$: Pretzel curve
steps.description: The ratio 3:2 produces an interlocking pretzel shape with 3 lobes horizontally and 2 vertically.

steps.title: Interactive: choose frequencies and phase
steps.description: Adjust $a$, $b$, and $\varphi$ to explore the full family of Lissajous curves.

scenes.prompt: Explain how frequency ratio a:b and phase phi control closure, symmetry, and lobe count. Encourage comparing rational ratios by switching camera views.

scenes.title: Building the Torus
scenes.description: A torus is built by sweeping a circle of radius $r$ along a circular spine of radius $R$. Two angles $\phi$ and $\theta$ locate any point on the surface.
scenes.markdown:
# Building the Torus

A **torus** is generated by revolving a circle of radius $r$ around an axis at distance $R$ from its centre:

$$\mathbf{r}(\phi, \theta) = \begin{pmatrix}(R + r\cos\theta)\cos\phi \\ (R + r\cos\theta)\sin\phi \\ r\sin\theta\end{pmatrix}, \quad \phi,\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$$

## The Two Parameters

| Symbol | Name | Role |
|--------|------|------|
| $R$ | Major radius | Distance from $z$-axis to tube centre |
| $r$ | Minor radius | Radius of the tube cross-section |
| $\phi$ | Longitude | Angle around the central spine circle |
| $\theta$ | Tube angle | Angle around the cross-section circle |

## Construction Steps

1. **Spine circle** — a circle of radius $R$ in the $xy$-plane
2. **Cross-section** — a circle of radius $r$ centred at $(R,0,0)$ in the $xz$-plane
3. **Sweep** — rotate the cross-section around the $z$-axis through angle $\phi \in [0,2\pi]$
4. **Complete torus** — the swept surface

Every point on the surface is reached by choosing exactly one $(\phi, \theta)$.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide isometric view of the torus

views.name: Top
views.description: Looking straight down the Y axis — see the torus ring from above

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view — see the torus cross-section

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view of the torus

steps.title: The spine circle
steps.description: The torus starts with a circle of radius $R=2$ in the $xy$-plane. This is the **spine** — the path that the centre of every cross-section will follow.

steps.title: The cross-section circle
steps.description: At the point $(R, 0, 0)$ on the spine, draw a small circle of radius $r = 0.6$ in the $xz$-plane. Every slice through the torus perpendicular to the spine is a copy of this circle.

steps.title: Sweeping halfway
steps.description: Rotate the cross-section circle around the $z$-axis from $\phi = 0$ to $\phi = \pi$. Each value of $\phi$ adds one ring to the surface, building up the first half of the torus.

steps.title: The complete torus
steps.description: Continue sweeping to $\phi = 2\pi$ to close the surface. Every point on the torus is now addressed by exactly two angles: $\phi$ (longitude, around the spine) and $\theta$ (tube angle, around the cross-section).

steps.title: Interactive: pick any point
steps.description: Drag $\phi$ and $\theta$ to move a point anywhere on the torus. The **red arrow $R$** runs from the origin to the centre of the cross-section at longitude $\phi$. The **orange arrow $r$** runs from that centre to your chosen point. The gold ring is the cross-section circle at $\phi$.

scenes.prompt: Teach the torus from construction primitives: spine circle + cross-section + sweep. Reinforce the roles of phi (longitude) and theta (tube angle).

scenes.title: Torus Knot
scenes.description: The $(2,3)$ torus knot winds 2 times around the torus the long way and 3 times through the hole, forming the trefoil knot.
scenes.markdown:
# The Torus Knot

A **torus knot** $T(p,q)$ is a curve that wraps around the surface of a torus:
- $p$ times in the **longitudinal** direction (around the big circle)
- $q$ times in the **meridional** direction (through the hole)

## Parametric Equations

The $(2,3)$ torus knot lives on a torus with major radius $R=2$:

$$x(t) = \bigl(2 + \cos(3t)\bigr)\cos(2t)$$
$$y(t) = \bigl(2 + \cos(3t)\bigr)\sin(2t)$$
$$z(t) = \sin(3t)$$

for $t \in [0, 2\pi]$.

## The Torus Surface

The underlying torus is parameterized by $(u, v) \in [0, 2\pi]^2$:

$$x = (2 + 0.6\cos v)\cos u, \quad y = (2 + 0.6\cos v)\sin u, \quad z = 0.6\sin v$$

The knot rides along this surface.

## Knot Theory

The $(2,3)$ torus knot is the **trefoil knot** — the simplest non-trivial knot.

- It **cannot** be untangled into a circle without cutting
- Its **crossing number** is 3 (minimum for any non-trivial knot)
- $\gcd(2,3) = 1$ ensures it is a single connected curve

When $\gcd(p,q) > 1$, you get a **torus link** with multiple components.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide isometric view of the torus knot

views.name: Top
views.description: Looking straight down the Y axis — see the winding pattern from above

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view — see the knot path face-on

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view of the torus knot

steps.title: The torus surface
steps.description: First the torus: major radius 2, minor radius 0.6. The knot will live on this surface.

steps.title: The trefoil knot in gold
steps.description: The $(2,3)$ torus knot wraps twice longitudinally and three times meridionally, closing back after $t = 2\pi$.

steps.title: First half of the knot
steps.description: The first half ($t \in [0,\pi]$) highlighted in pink. Notice it crosses over and under the second half, creating the trefoil's 3 crossings.

steps.title: Interactive: lift the knot
steps.description: Drag the slider to change the knot's wrapping radius $s$. At $s = 0.6$ the knot rides exactly on the torus surface. Increase $s$ and it peels away into free space — still the same trefoil topology, but no longer confined to the torus.

steps.title: Interactive: explore T(p, q)
steps.description: Slide $p$ and $q$ to change the winding numbers. **$p$** counts how many times the curve goes the long way around the torus; **$q$** counts trips through the hole. When $\gcd(p,q)=1$ you get a true knot. When they share a common factor $d = \gcd(p,q)$, the curve closes after only $1/d$ of its range — you have a $d$-component link rather than a knot. Try $p=2, q=3$ (trefoil), then $p=2, q=4$ (2-component link), then $p=3, q=6$ (3 loops).

scenes.prompt: Explain torus-knot winding intuition and how amplitude changes embedding without changing knot type. Distinguish knot vs link behavior conceptually.

scenes.title: Knot on a Torus
scenes.description: A torus knot lives on the surface of a torus. Drag the slider to see the knot swell from touching the surface to looping freely in space.
scenes.markdown:
# Knot on a Torus

A **torus knot** $T(p,q)$ is a curve that wraps around the surface of a torus without crossing itself:

$$\mathbf{r}(t) = \bigl((R + r\cos qt)\cos pt,\;(R + r\cos qt)\sin pt,\;r\sin qt\bigr)$$

when the wrapping amplitude $r$ equals the torus tube radius, the knot lies **exactly on the surface**.

## On the Surface vs. Off

If we scale the meridional amplitude independently from the tube radius, the knot can:

| $s$ | Position |
|-----|----------|
| $s < r$ | Inside the torus tube |
| $s = r = 0.6$ | Exactly on the surface |
| $s > r$ | Lifted above the surface |

The topology of the knot is unchanged — it is still a trefoil, still $T(2,3)$ — only its embedding in space changes.

## Why Tori?

The torus is the natural habitat for this family of curves because:
- Longitude and meridian provide **two independent winding directions**
- The curve closes when $t = 2\pi$ (if $\gcd(p,q)=1$)
- The winding numbers $(p,q)$ completely classify the knot type

steps.title: The torus surface
steps.description: Major radius $R=2$, tube radius $r=0.6$. Every point on the surface is specified by two angles: $u$ going around the big circle, $v$ going around the tube.

steps.title: T(2, 3) trefoil on the surface
steps.description: The trefoil knot $T(2,3)$ sits exactly on the torus. Each time it goes around the big circle it also completes part of a loop through the hole, weaving over and under itself 3 times.

steps.title: Interactive: lift the knot off the surface
steps.description: The slider $s$ controls the knot's meridional amplitude independently of the torus tube radius. At $s=0.6$ the knot is flush with the surface. Pull it higher and the knot detaches, becoming a free-floating trefoil in space — same topological type, different geometric embedding.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide isometric view of the knot on the torus

views.name: Top Down
views.description: Looking straight down from above

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view — see the knot path

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view

scenes.prompt: Connect the torus surface and knot embedding, emphasizing how slider s moves between on-surface and lifted embeddings while preserving topology.
